context:
- Pattern: untimed work is fine; timed drills reveal drops in accuracy and speed.
- Tactics to convert untimed competence into timed accuracy and speed:
  - Threshold rule: don’t time a topic until you can solve representative problems untimed correctly 4–5 times in a row; then introduce timed drills.
  - Micro‑timing drills: break a full problem into steps with time targets (e.g., 30s read/triage, 90s setup, Xs computation, 15s answer check).
  - Timed re‑solve: after an untimed correct solution, re‑solve the same problem under a shorter timer.
  - Mixed short sets: many short (10–30 minute) timed sets that force frequent triage/decision practice.
  - Triage rules & skip strategy: quick checklist for deciding when to skip/mark and return; practice applying it.
  - Error‑type drills: separate careless/timing errors from conceptual errors.
  - Use your one‑page sheet: practice finding items in <5s during timed drills.
  - Reduce cognitive overhead: standardize abbreviations, templates, and a short mental script at problem start.
  - Track metrics: log timed accuracy, average time/question, and time distribution by section.
  - Recovery and pacing: morning‑of exam routine and short in‑exam resets (deep breath, 10s checklist).
- Availability: I can convert these into a short set of timed drills for weekday 2‑hour blocks and weekend sessions if desired.
- Latest performance baseline: full mock matched the exam split with ~70% knowledge and mid‑50s applied.
- If adjusting a 6‑week plan: provide weekend hours (per day or total), specify weekdays with evening shifts, and state a target score or pass threshold; I’ll allocate more weekend deep applied practice and weekday micro timed drills to close the gap.
